coredata.dc:title: Molecular manipulation of PEDOT:PSS for efficient hole transport by incorporation of N-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Duong Nguyen
Roh, Seung Hun
Kim, Dong-Hwan
Lee, Jun Young
Wang, Dong Hwan
Kim, Jung Kyu
coredata.dc:description: Solution-processed poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) films have great potential for organic optoelectronic applications due to their high throughput, low cost, and mechanical flexibility. However, the strong acidity and insulating nature of PSS causes a crucial deterioration of device performance. Here, we present an efficient route for addressing the limitations of PSS by molecular manipulation via incorporation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) into PEDOT:PSS. The incorporation of N-CQDs significantly reduces resistance, increases work function, modifies the surface energy and smooths the surface morphology of PEDOT:PSS. In our density functional theory (DFT) calculations, inclusion of N-CQDs weakens the electrostatic interaction between PEDOT and PSS, resulting in molecular manipulation of PEDOT:PSS. Compared to conventionally oxidized CQDs (O-CQDs), N-CQDs have better effects in PEDOT:PSS matrices, including π−π stacking with PEDOT and hydrophilic interaction with PSS, which can effectively induce molecular reorientation. The enhancement of charge extraction and charge injection performance of N-CQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS are demonstrated via organic optoelectronic devices including organic photovoltaics and organic light-emitting diode with significant improvements in power conversion efficiency and quantum efficiency, respectively. Molecular manipulation via the incorporation of N-CQDs holds promise for the development of highly efficient PEDOT:PSS hole-transporting layers in various optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot
Density functional theory calculation
Hole transport layer
Organic photovoltaic
Organic light-emitting diode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Preparation of oxidized carbon quantum dots (O-CQDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning
Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Korea Institute of Industrial Technology as “Development of smart textronic products based on electronic fibers and textiles (kitech JA-21-0001 )”. This study was conducted with the support of the Gyeongi-Do Technology Development Program as “Development of smart textronic products based on electronic fibers and textiles (kitech IZ-21-0001 ). This research was also supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Science , ICT (MSIT) ( 2019R1A2C1087653 ).

This study was supported by the Korea Institute of Industrial Technology as ?Development of smart textronic products based on electronic fibers and textiles (kitech JA-21-0001)?. This study was conducted with the support of the Gyeongi-Do Technology Development Program as ?Development of smart textronic products based on electronic fibers and textiles (kitech IZ-21-0001). This research was also supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT (MSIT) (2019R1A2C1087653).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution histogram, and (c) HRTEM image of the synthesized N-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of the synthesized N-CQDs. (b) UV–Vis spectra, (c) PL spectra and (d) PLE spectra of N-CQDs. XPS survey scan of N-CQDs: (e) C1s and (f) N1s.
(a) XPS S(2p), (b) UPS, and (c) Raman spectra of PEDOT:PSS film before and after incorporation of N-CQD.
(a,b) AFM images of pristine PEDOT:PSS and N-CQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS thin film. (c,d) Contact angle between water droplet and (N-CQD)/PEDOT:PSS substrate.

Theoretical DFT calculation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and oxidized carbon quantum dots (O-CQDs). (a) Optimized structures of N-CQD (top), O-CQD (bottom) (C, N, O, H atoms are shown in grey, blue, red, and white, respectively). Potential energy scan for each pair of (b) CQD/PEDOT and (c) CQD/PSS models in the range from 7.0 Å to 2.0 Å. (d) Comparative PEDOT-to-PSS distance at different models. The interaction distance is measured from center-to-center of each fragment, rather than atom-to-atom. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

OPV device performance and characterization. (a) Energy band diagram of the OPV device. (b) Current vs. potential (J–V) curves (inset: OPV device with the structure ITO/PEDOT:PSS/active layer/TiOx/Al), (c) Photovoltaic response, and (d) EQE spectra of OPV devices.

OLED device performance and characterization. (a) Log-log plots of the current density-to-voltage characteristics of hole-only devices composed of ITO/(N-CQD)PEDOT:PSS/PVK/Au. (b) J–V characteristic and luminance curves. (c) Quantum efficiency plotted as a function of luminance. (d) EL spectra of OLED with/without N-CQD (inset: light emission from OLED device taken at a driving voltage of 5 V in the dark).

head.ce:title: Molecular manipulation of PEDOT:PSS for efficient hole transport by incorporation of N-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Sungkyunkwan University (SKKU), Suwon, 16419, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Sungkyunkwan University (SKKU), Suwon, 16419, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Integrative Engineering, Chung-Ang University, Seoul, 06974, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Integrative Engineering, Chung-Ang University, Seoul, 06974, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solution-processed poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) films have great potential for organic optoelectronic applications due to their high throughput, low cost, and mechanical flexibility. However, the strong acidity and insulating nature of PSS causes a crucial deterioration of device performance. Here, we present an efficient route for addressing the limitations of PSS by molecular manipulation via incorporation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) into PEDOT:PSS. The incorporation of N-CQDs significantly reduces resistance, increases work function, modifies the surface energy and smooths the surface morphology of PEDOT:PSS. In our density functional theory (DFT) calculations, inclusion of N-CQDs weakens the electrostatic interaction between PEDOT and PSS, resulting in molecular manipulation of PEDOT:PSS. Compared to conventionally oxidized CQDs (O-CQDs), N-CQDs have better effects in PEDOT:PSS matrices, including π−π stacking with PEDOT and hydrophilic interaction with PSS, which can effectively induce molecular reorientation. The enhancement of charge extraction and charge injection performance of N-CQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS are demonstrated via organic optoelectronic devices including organic photovoltaics and organic light-emitting diode with significant improvements in power conversion efficiency and quantum efficiency, respectively. Molecular manipulation via the incorporation of N-CQDs holds promise for the development of highly efficient PEDOT:PSS hole-transporting layers in various optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Highly conductive poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) has been studied as a hole injection/transport material for optoelectronic devices due to its good transparency in the visible range (85% for 100 nm thickness), low-temperature solution processability, suitable work function (5.2 eV) and mechanical flexibility [1–6]. However, the intrinsically acidic nature of PEDOT:PSS causes deterioration of the top organic active layer, while its inhomogeneous conductivity and high surface roughness reduce the performance of the devices [7–10]. Therefore, many attempts have been made to overcome the drawbacks of PEDOT:PSS thin films. In PEDOT:PSS, the stabilizer PSS, which is used to disperse PEDOT in solvents, but it is an electrical insulator with high acidity. Thus, decreasing the PSS component in the synthetic process or removing the excess PSS using high-dielectric solvents have been suggested as possible approaches to improve the performance of the system [11–13]. To date, several methods have been developed to modify the intrinsic properties of PEDOT:PSS; these methods use organic solvents such as ethylene glycol (EG) [14], N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) [15], sorbitol [16], ionic liquid [17], or post-treatment with dimethyl sulfoxide and p-toluenesulfonic acid (PTSA) [18]. When PEDOT:PSS is used as a hole transport layer (HTL) in optoelectronic devices such as organic photovoltaics (OPVs) or organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs), efficient charge transport in the multi-layered structure including organic/organic or metal/organic interfaces is considered necessary for the success of system operation [19–21]. The introduction of organic solvents can drastically improve the electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS [22]; however, the electrical conductivity is not the only key factor to improve device performance. For example, the treatment of PEDOT:PSS with EG produces higher electrical conductivity, but also increases the surface roughness, resulting in a decrease in the performance of organic optoelectronics due to the presence of defects or traps that facilitate recombination of the surface charge carriers [14]. Besides, the removal of PSS from PEDOT:PSS using sulfonic acid-containing molecules strongly induces PEDOT aggregation and film etching [18].

Another approach to improving PEDOT:PSS-based devices involves the use of nanocarbons, including graphene, fullerene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots, as an additive in PEDOT:PSS. Such additives modify the PSS component in the PEDOT:PSS mixture and provide an alternative electrically conductive pathway within PEDOT:PSS matrices [1,6,23]. Nanocarbons are useful for optoelectronic applications due to their high conductivity and tunable band edges, which depend on their size and chemical functionality and are essential to improving the efficiency of the resulting devices [24–27]. The chemical as well as electronic structure of nanocarbons is critical for such applications because these determine the hydrophobic/hydrophilic nature and electrical conductivity of the material. For example, while graphene oxide (GO) acts as an insulator with excellent hydrophilicity [28–30], reduction of GO can convert it to a semimetal with hydrophobic properties [31–33]. In our previous study, the introduction of graphene oxide quantum dots with hydrophilic functional groups into hydrophilic PEDOT:PSS resulted in an enhancement of the power conversion efficiency of OPVs due to the modified morphological properties and reduced resistance of the PEDOT:PSS film [34,35]. Previous studies indicated that the structure and properties of PEDOT:PSS films are sensitive to the nature of the introduced nanocarbons [26,34,35]. However, the mechanism behind the effect of incorporated CQDs on the molecular structure of PEDOT:PSS films has not yet been clarified. Understanding the mechanism of improved performance via a combination of experimental data and theoretical calculations is very important for molecular science as well as practical applications of the devices.

In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were incorporated into PEDOT:PSS HTL to achieve efficient charge transport performance in organic optoelectronic devices including OPV and OLED. The incorporated N-CQDs induced molecular manipulation and reorientation, and ameliorated the morphological properties of PEDOT:PSS, resulting in improvements in the electrical conductivity, work function and surface energy, and a smoother surface. Reorientation of PEDOT and PSS modulates the charge migration behavior and mobility of PEDOT:PSS HTL [36,37]. In our density functional theory (DFT) calculations, the incorporated N-CQDs weaken the electrostatic interaction between PEDOT and PSS, resulting in tailoring of the molecular interactions between PEDOT and PSS. Compared to carbon quantum dots with oxygen-related functional groups (O-CQDs), N-CQDs have better effects on PEDOT:PSS matrices, including π−π stacking with PEDOT and hydrophilic interaction with PSS, which effectively induce molecular reorientation. In order to demonstrate the enhancements in charge extraction and charge injection performance, N-CQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS was introduced to organic photovoltaics (OPVs) and organic light-emitting diode (OLED) devices as the hole transporting layer. As a result, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the N-CQD-incorporated OPV was significantly improved, with a noticeable increase in VOC, JSC, and FF parameters compared to those of pristine PEDOT:PSS-based OPV and O-CQD-incorporated OPV devices. Regarding its application in OLEDs, the introduction of N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS in an OLED device resulted in a significant enhancement in quantum efficiency compared to that of the device using pristine PEDOT:PSS. The ternary N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS system as a hole transport/injection material may complement the shortcomings of both of the individual materials. This study investigated the great potential of N-CQD incorporation into PEDOT:PSS in OPV and OLED devices.

Fumaronitrile was purchased from TCI chemicals, Tokyo, Japan. Carbon nanofiber was supplied by Sigma Aldrich, Seoul, Korea. PEDOT:PSS (Clevios P VP AI4083) was provided by Heraeus, Ossila Co., UK. Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 wt%) and nitric acid (HNO3, 63 wt%) were acquired from Samchun Co., Korea. Other chemicals were purchased from Sigma Aldrich or Acros (Seoul, Korea) and used without any purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, fumaronitrile (300 mg, 3.84 mmol) was sonicated in tetrahydrofuran (3 mL) for 30 min before being added to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Solvothermal treatment was carried out at 200 °C for 20 min. After cooling the autoclave down to room temperature, the resulting material was filtered (0.2 μm, PTFE membrane), centrifuged several times and re-dispersed in ethanol. Solid N-CQDs were obtained by a rotary evaporator for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of oxidized carbon quantum dots (O-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: O-CQDs were synthesized via a facile oxidation method using carbon nanofiber as a precursor, as previously reported [35]. Carbon nanofiber (0.5 g) was slowly added to a mixture of HNO3 (20 mL) and H2SO4 (60 mL). The solution was stirred at 120 °C for 24 h. Afterward, the solution was diluted with deionized water (800 mL) and neutralized by Na2CO3 to obtain a neutral pH (~6.7). The as-prepared O-CQDs were then dialyzed for 3 days using a 2000 Da dialysis bag. To collect solid O-CQDs, a freeze-drying process was carried out.

The PEDOT:PSS solution was first filtered through a PVDF membrane (0.45 μm pore size) to remove large particles. The appropriate amount of CQD was added to the PEDOT:PSS solution (weight percentage of CQD, 3 wt%) and sonicated for 30 min, then stirred for 1 h to produce a homogeneous solution. Afterward, the dispersions were spin-coated onto pre-patterned indium tin oxide (ITO) on glass (cleaned by sequential ultrasonication in DI water for 10 min, isopropanol for 10 min, acetone for 10 min, and UV ozone treatment for 10 min) at 4000 rpm for 40 s. The substrate was then dried at 150 °C for 15 min on a hot plate to evaporate water and stored in a vacuum oven to prohibit undesired moisture adsorption.

An ITO/hole transport layer (HTL)/active layer (PTB7:PC71BM)/TiOx/Al device configuration was used to investigate the effect of the incorporation of CQDs into the HTL on charge transport in the resulting solar cell devices. The CQD/PEDOT:PSS thin film on the ITO glass substrate prepared above was used as the HTL. PTB7:PC71BM (1:0.8 w/w) chlorobenzene solution (2.0 wt%) was spin-coated onto the HTL at 2000 rpm for 30 s in a glove box and then annealed at 150 °C for 10 min to obtain the active layer. Finally, TiOx and Al as the electron transport layer and cathode were thermally evaporated under 10−6 torr. The active area was 4 mm2. The thickness of each layer was controlled by spin coating conditions; the thicknesses were 35, 80, 6, and 200 nm for PEDOT:PSS, PTB7:PC71BM, TiOx, and Al, respectively. A similar process was carried out to fabricate organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) with PEDOT:PSS, poly(N–vinylcarbazole) (PVK), TCTA:mCP-PFP/Ir(mppy)3, LiF and Al layers.

The sheet resistance (RS) of the PEDOT:PSS films was measured by the Van der Pauw four-point probe technique with a Keithley 2400 source meter, using the equation below:
R S
=
π
ln
( 2 )
Δ V
I
= 4.532
x
Δ V
I

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a Varian Cary FLR025 spectrometer (Agilent). XPS data were acquired with an ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Scientific) spectrometer with an Al-Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). UPS measurements were performed in a homemade in situ photoemission analysis system (generator: VUV 5000; detector: SES-100, hn = 40.8 eV, He II source). AFM images were acquired using a Veeco NanoScope IV AFM operated in tapping mode. TEM and HR-TEM images were obtained with a JEM-2100F (JEOL) microscope. Raman spectra were conducted using a NanoBase XperRam 200 spectrophotometer with a 532 nm excitation laser. XRD patterns were acquired using a Bruker D8 Advance XRD Instrument. FT-IR spectra were acquired with an IFS-66/S, TENSOR27 (Bruker).

DFT calculations were executed to determine the electronic structures of the CQDs using ORCA 4.1 and Gaussian 09 software [38,39]. The DFT calculations were conducted using B3LYP functional and a balanced polarized triple-ζ Ahlrichs def2 basis set, namely, def2-TZVP. The dispersion forces were employed using Grimme's DFT-D3 method for dispersion correction. Octamer molecules were constructed as miniature models of the polymers, and ethyl groups were used as termination points instead of long side chains. All calculations were performed in a vacuum environment. The interaction energy of CQDs with polymers was calculated with the following equation:
Eint = Eab(CQD/polymer) – [Eab(CQD) + Eab(polymer)]

The N-CQDs were synthesized via a one-step short-time solvothermal method by using fumaronitrile as nitrogen-containing precursor. Due to the oxygen-free nature of fumaronitrile, the oxygen content in the as-synthesized N-CQD can be controlled by varying the solvent in synthesis. The morphology of N-CQDs was characterized by TEM, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The dots were circular and showed good dispersion. According to the size distribution histogram (Fig. 1b), the majority of the particle sizes were well-distributed, with diameters in the range of 2–5 nm and an average particle size of 3.32 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image in Fig. 1c suggests an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, while the XRD pattern of N-CQDs in Fig. S1 presents a broad single peak centered at 2θ = 20°. These findings are in agreement with previous reports [24,40]. The dispersed suspension of N-CQDs in water is quite stable and can be stored for months at room temperature (25 °C) without any precipitation.

The functional groups of N-CQDs were characterized using FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 a). The major vibration peaks appear at 3444, 1629, 1411, and 1049 cm−1, which are attributed to N–H/O–H bonds, CC/CN bonds, C–H bonds, and asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C–C bonds, respectively [41,42]. Additionally, the appearance of vibration peaks at 2206 cm−1 indicates the existence of a nitrile group (−CN sp1) [41,43]. The appearance of oxygen-containing groups suggests an interaction between N-CQDs and moisture in ambient conditions.

The as-synthesized N-CQD solutions are light yellowish in an atmospheric environment, while blue photoluminescence emission was seen following excitation with 365 nm UV light. When absorption is plotted against wavelength, as shown in Fig. 2b, three major absorption bands centered at 225, 295 and 385 nm are found for N-CQDs in various dilutions, corresponding to π → π* of C–C, n → π* transition of C–N, and C–O bonds, respectively [43]. Fig. 2c shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the N-CQD solutions according to the tuning excitation wavelength (λ ex). PL intensity and maximal emission wavelengths (λ em) are strongly dependent on λ ex. By increasing λ ex from 340 to 420 nm, the peak position of λ em shows a red shift, which was previously observed for CQDs [26,44]. Moreover, the maximized peak intensity (emission at 468 nm) occurs with a λ ex of about 380 nm. With a further increase to λ ex > 440 nm, no emission peaks are detected. This result suggests that the corresponding CQDs are sensitive to the incident wavelength in the range of 320–420 nm, corresponding to excitation energy traps of the main nitrogen-containing groups [43]. In the corresponding photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum in Fig. 2d, there are two excitation bands located at 285 and 380 nm. This result is in agreement with the UV–Vis and PL emission spectra of N-CQD solution.

To analyze the chemical structure of N-CQDs, XPS survey spectra were carried out (Fig. S2). The full-scan XPS spectra of the N-CQDs show two main peaks at 285 and 400 eV, which are consistent with C1s and N1s, respectively. The C1s spectra (Fig. 2e) presents two major peaks at 284.7 (C–C) and 285.5 (C–N) eV, and a minor peak at 286.4 (C–O) eV. The N1s spectra (Fig. 2f) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.7, 400.0 and 401.1 eV, which correspond to pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen, respectively.

We further performed XPS measurements of PEDOT:PSS films with and without N-CQD incorporation to probe whether N-CQD doping affects the inner composition on the surface of the PEDOT:PSS film. The S(2p) spectra of the PEDOT:PSS and N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS films are compared in Fig. 3 a. The spectra exhibit two bands at 162–166 and 166–171 eV, which are assigned to the sulfur signals from the oxidized form of thiophene (in PEDOT chains) and sulfonate (in PSS chains), respectively [45–47]. After incorporation of N-CQD, the ratio of PEDOT to PSS increases from 1:7.73 to 1:3.68, potentially because the carbon domains disturb the sulfur signal from PSS while the PEDOT and PSS chains are well-detached because of their strong interaction with N-CQDs. The reduction of the Coulombic interaction between PEDOT and PSS chains induces phase separation on the nanometer scale, which facilitates the decrease in the portion of PSS chain at the film surface. That decrease in the portion of PSS is expected to affect the work function, surface morphology, surface energy, and electrical conductivity of the films [2,3]. In detail, the work function of the PEDOT:PSS film (as obtained by UV photoelectron spectroscopy, Fig. 3b) is tunable, with values of −5.21 eV and −5.08 eV before and after the addition of N-CQDs, respectively. Fig. 3c shows the Raman spectra of pristine and N-CQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS thin film. Obviously, the band between 1400 and 1500 cm−1, which corresponding to Cα = Cβ symmetric stretching vibration, presents a red shift after introducing N-CQD into PEDOT:PSS matrix. The shift from 1454 to 1440 cm−1 indicates the resonant structure of the PEDOT chain changes from a benzoid structure with favorable coil conformation to a quinoid structure with preferable linear or expanded-coil conformation [48–50]. This implies that the incorporation of N-CQD induces the conformational change of PEDOT chain in PEDOT:PSS matrix. To estimate the charge transport capabilities of the (N-CQD/)PEDOT:PSS composite interface, the sheet resistance (RS) of each sample was investigated by the four-point probe method. Accordingly, the RS value of PEDOT:PSS decreased from 380 to 220 kΩ/cm2 after introduction of N-CQDs. The increase in conductivity of the N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS sample can be explained by the formation of more conductive pathways via reorientation of PEDOT in the PEDOT:PSS matrix upon addition of N-CQDs.

The addition of N-CQDs also affects the surface morphology of the PEDOT:PSS film. High-resolution 3D AFM images were obtained to allow assessment of possible changes in surface morphology following incorporation of N-CQDs into PEDOT:PSS. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the root-mean-square (RMS) roughness of PEDOT:PSS and N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS were found to be 1.344 nm and 0.761 nm, respectively. The surface morphology of PEDOT:PSS containing N-CQDs is relatively homogeneous and consists of interconnected PEDOT-rich domains with a low RMS value [51]. The transmittance of the spin-coated N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS films on ITO substrates is another critical factor which strongly affects the device performance. The introduction of N-CQD into the PEDOT:PSS matrix does not notably alter the transparency of the electrode, which has a transmittance value of more than 82% in the visible range (450–800 nm) (Fig. S3).

Fig. 4c and d shows the contact angles between water droplets and the N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS surface. As is evident in the figures, the contact angle of water on the PEDOT:PSS surface is around 9.5°, suggesting that the pristine PEDOT:PSS surface has excellent wettability. On the other hand, following incorporation of N-CQDs into PEDOT:PSS, the film surface becomes hydrophobic, with a contact angle of 21.4°. The increase in the contact angle suggests a reduction in wettability of the polymer surface due to the dominance of hydrophobic PEDOT-rich domains in the surface, as mentioned above.

We next propose a model to explain the formation of the N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS composite. The abovementioned experimental results provide reliable evidence for the existence of strong interactions between N-CQDs and both PEDOT and PSS chains at the molecular level, leading to a phase separation between PEDOT and PSS. It is well-established that the PEDOT chains in the structure of PEDOT:PSS have abundant π bonds, while N-CQDs have plenty of π bonds, so the latter could be embedded into a PEDOT:PSS matrix by strong π-π interactions between N-CQDs and PEDOT chains. In addition, due to the nitrogen-containing groups in N-CQDs, they also interact with PSS chains via hydrophilic-hydrophilic interactions. Therefore, N-CQDs not only connect PEDOT chains tightly but also promote orderly alignment of PEDOT chains on the surface of N-CQDs via π-π interaction, which significantly enhances the electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS. Meanwhile, PSS detaches from PEDOT and wraps around the edges of N-CQDs via the assembly of hydrophilic groups between N-CQDs and PSS chains. Hence, these strong multiphase interactions induce a phase separation between the PEDOT and PSS chains, which has previously been reported elsewhere [52–55]. Consequently, the ordered assembly of PEDOT and N-CQDs provides for fast carrier transport and would thus be expected to improve device performance.

To better understand the interaction between N-CQDs and polymers in PEDOT:PSS matrices, we performed DFT calculations and compared the N-CQD results with the results of other conventional CQDs such as O-CQDs and pristine CQDs (Fig. S4). Before the interaction energy between CQDs and polymers was calculated, each isolated CQD was geometrically optimized in vacuum conditions (Fig. 5 a). During the geometrical optimization process, each N-CQD slowly bends to minimize the conformational energy due to the termination of nitrogen atoms at pyridinic/pyrrolic positions. As a result, an optimized N-CQD shows a relatively high degree of curvature, with a curvature angle of ≈36°. Meanwhile, an O-CQD with terminated oxygen-containing groups shows an almost planar structure with a tiny curvature angle of less than 5°. This conformation is the same as that of a pristine CQD, which has a curvature angle of nearly zero (Fig. S4a). The high curvature of N-CQDs thermodynamically favors adsorption to a polymer. Besides, the termination by nitrogen is more efficient than by oxygen in charge distribution as well as frontier molecular orbitals (Fig. S5).

A potential energy scan was performed on each CQD/polymer pair to estimate the dependence of the interaction energy on the CQD-to-polymer distance. The interaction energy was calculated using the following equation:
E(r)int = E(CQD/polymer)a – [E(CQD)a + E(polymer)a]

Here, E(r)int is the interaction energy at distance r, and E(CQD)a, E(polymer)a, and E(CQD/polymer)a are the absolute energies of the model CQD, polymer, and the CQD/polymer ensemble, respectively, taken from a single-point energy estimated on the geometries optimized at distance r. It should be noted that if the interaction energy is negative, attraction is the dominant force, while in the opposite case, repulsion dominates. Fig. 5b and c clearly shows how the interaction between CQDs and either PEDOT or PSS varies as a function of the separation distance. In the case of PEDOT, all CQDs show good adsorption on the polymer due to π-π stacking with negative interaction energy values when the distance is small enough. The optimized distance between N-CQD or O-CQD and PEDOT is 3.6 and 4.2 Å with typical adsorption energies of −0.26 and −0.22 eV, respectively. Also, in the case of PSS, N-CQD exhibits a reasonable interaction with the polymer in comparison with O-CQD. Furthermore, a separation between PEDOT and PSS was achieved by weakening of the PEDOT-to-PSS interaction (Fig. 5d). The distance between PEDOT and PSS after introduction of N-CQD (or O-CQD) increases from 7.6 to 10.2 (or 9.3) Å. Pristine CQD had much less ability to break the PEDOT:PSS interaction, with an increase in r of just 0.4 Å (Fig. S4d).

Given the discussion above, functionalizing of CQD with highly electronegative atoms greatly weakens the PEDOT:PSS interaction. The DFT calculations show that N-CQD more strongly interacts with PEDOT and PSS chains than O-CQD, thus better inducing a phase separation between PEDOT and PSS and promoting the ordered alignment of PEDOT chains. On the other hand, the reduction of O-CQD into pristine CQD produces no separation of PEDOT and PSS (Figs. S4d–f). Instead, according to previous works, a new electrically conductive pathway is achieved by incorporation of pristine CQD within PEDOT:PSS matrices [34,35].

CQD/PEDOT:PSS thin films were used as the HTL in PTB7:PC71BM BHJ OPVs fabricated by solution-based spin coating on an indium-tin oxide (ITO)-coated glass substrate. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the band diagram of the OPV devices indicates the change in work function (WF) of HTL after introduction of N-CQD into PEDOT:PSS matrices. The tuned WF of N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS (5.08 eV) is more suitable for the donor material (PTB7), in which the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) is at 5.15 eV [56].

The current−voltage (J−V) curves of devices containing different CQDs are compared in Fig. 6b, including those of reference devices with N-CQDs and devices with O-CQDs for comparison. The XPS data of O-CQD (Fig. S6) reveals that carbonyl and carboxyl groups mainly dominate the chemical structure. The measured parameters of the OPV device, including open-circuit voltage (VOC), short-circuit current (JSC), fill factor (FF), and power conversion efficiency (PCE), are summarized in Table S1. The VOC value differs somewhat among the OPVs, and the ohmic contact of the anode/active layer was maintained in the presence of CQDs. Although the change in VOC is not significant due to the small amount of introduced CQDs in the devices, the VOC value of the N-CQD-based device is somewhat higher than those of the other devices. On the other hand, the increase in FF, which is determined by the charge carriers reaching the electrodes, is similar for the N-CQD- and O-CQD-based devices, which may be attributable to the improved electrode/active layer interface. The J−V characteristics clearly show that the BHJ-OPVs with the N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS film exhibited the best PCE. In detail, the reference device with N-CQDs shows a relatively high PCE of 8.57% calculated from a VOC of 0.74 V, a JSC of 17.2 mA/cm2, and a FF of 66.63%. This high performance (compared to the performance of the device with pristine PEDOT:PSS film) can be attributed to the tailored work function, increased electrical conductivity and smoother surface caused by incorporation of N-CQDs, which strongly affect the contact resistance at the interface and the leakage currents. Importantly, the N-CQD-based device showed improvements in many parameters compared to the O-CQD-based device, demonstrating the superiority caused by incorporation of N-CQDs into the HTL. Indeed, use of N-CQDs leads to a 17% increase in the PCE, compared to a 12% enhancement when O-CQDs were used in the PEDOT:PSS HTL. A comparison of the measured parameters is shown in Fig. 5c.

In order to assess the photovoltaic properties of the devices from J−V measurements, we further measured the external quantum efficiency (EQE) of the corresponding devices, as shown in Fig. 6d. The device based on N-CQDs exhibits a good photon response in the visible range, thus resulting in high photoelectric conversion efficiency, which coincides with the values from the J-V curves. Compared to the O-CQD-based device, the N-CQD-based device shows a much higher EQE across the wavelength region from 550 nm.

We further applied N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS thin film to OLEDs to produce excellent p-type conductivity of the hole injection layers (HILs) [41,57]. The OLEDs basically consisted of PEDOT:PSS multilayer films, poly(N–vinylcarbazole) (PVK), host-guest polymer, LiF and Al, which were sequentially deposited on ITO glass substrates by solution processing as the HIL, HTL, luminescence layer, electron injection layer, and cathode, respectively. The emitting luminescence layer (EML) was composed of TCTA:mCP-PFP host polymer (9:1, 0.7 wt%) and emitting dopants, Ir(mppy)3 (10 wt%). The thickness of PEDOT:PSS, PVK, LiF and Al were ca. 35, 10, 0.5, and 200 nm, respectively.

The hole mobility properties in the HTL were assessed by measurement of a hole-only device (HOD) composed of ITO/(N-CQD)PEDOT:PSS/PVK/Au. As shown in Fig. 7 a, after incorporation of N-CQD, the hole currents of HOD were significantly increased, indicating an improvement in hole mobility. Considering the large injection barrier of PEDOT:PSS, the higher hole current reveals that the incorporation of N-CQD improved the electrical conductivity and surface properties of PEDOT:PSS film which were strongly correlated with the hole mobility and charge migration behavior in the device. The electrical and electroluminescence (EL) properties of the OLEDs with and without N-CQDs were characterized and compared using current density-voltage-luminance (J-V-L) curves and EL spectra, respectively. As shown by the J-V curves in Fig. 7b, the N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS sample exhibited better electrical rectification than the PEDOT:PSS sample, with electrical thresholds of 5.5 and 6.5 V, respectively. Notably, compared to pristine PEDOT:PSS, the N-CQD-incorporated sample showed a steeper increase in current density at forward applied voltages, revealing the superior diode characteristics of N-CQDs/PEDOT:PSS-based OLEDs. The N-CQD-incorporated OLED shows significantly greater luminance (Fig. 7b) and luminous quantum efficiency (QE) above the electrical thresholds (Fig. 7c) than the OLED without N-CQD. Introduction of N-CQDs resulted in an increase in QE from 12.1% to 13.6% at 1000 cd/m2. The EL emission properties of the OLEDs were further characterized by EL spectroscopic analysis. Fig. 7d shows the effect of incorporation of N-CQDs into the OLED on the emission intensity. Both samples show a similar dominant emission peak at 511 nm under applied voltage, regardless of the presence of N-CQDs. The insignificant enhancement in intensity indicates that the EL emission is mostly attributable to radiative electron-hole recombination at the EML despite N-CQD incorporation. The inset in Fig. 7d shows a 2.0 × 2.0-mm2 chip of the OLED, which exhibits uniform light emission over the entire chip area.

In summary, an effective route for enhancing the hole transporting performance of PEDOT:PSS film by incorporation of N-CQDs was demonstrated. Incorporation of the N-CQDs induced molecular reorientation and ameliorated the morphological properties of the PEDOT:PSS hole transport layer. The enhanced charge transport performance of N-CQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS resulted from improvements in the electrical conductivity, work function, and surface energy, as well as creation of a smoother surface. Theoretical calculations revealed that the inclusion of N-CQDs weakens the electrostatic interactions between PEDOT and PSS, thus tailoring the molecular interactions between PEDOT and PSS. Compared to O-CQD, with its oxygen-related functional groups, N-CQD is superior in PEDOT:PSS matrices as it is capable of π−π stacking with PEDOT and hydrophilic interaction with PSS, which effectively promote molecular reorientation. In order to demonstrate the enhancements of charge extraction and charge injection performance, the N-CQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS was introduced into the organic optoelectronic devices including organic photovoltaic (OPV) and organic light-emitting diode (OLED) as the hole transporting layer. As a result, the overall PCE of the N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS-incorporated OPV had a noticeably higher VOC and JSC and achieved the highest FF value (8.57%) which was greater than that of O-CQD/PEDOT:PSS (8.14%) and pristine PEDOT:PSS (7.26%). Regarding its application in OLEDs, the introduction of N-CQD/PEDOT:PSS in an OLED resulted in a significant enhancement in quantum efficiency compared to that of the device containing pristine PEDOT:PSS. Given these benefits, molecular manipulation through incorporation of N-CQD in PEDOT:PSS shows great potential for the creation of organic optoelectronic devices with high performance.

Duong Nguyen Nguyen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Seung Hun Roh: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Dong-Hwan Kim: Conceptualization, Investigation. Jun Young Lee: Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources. Dong Hwan Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Jung Kyu Kim: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Korea Institute of Industrial Technology as “Development of smart textronic products based on electronic fibers and textiles (kitech JA-21-0001)”. This study was conducted with the support of the Gyeongi-Do Technology Development Program as “Development of smart textronic products based on electronic fibers and textiles (kitech IZ-21-0001). This research was also supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT (MSIT) (2019R1A2C1087653).
